nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon areas, lower reef slopes, and turbid reef environments with subdued lighting and moderate water movement

decorPreferences: spacing of at least 5 cm from neighboring corals
stable substrate to attach and encrust

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Target feeding with small meaty foods (mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, reef roids, or similar coral foods) once to twice per week has been reported to promote faster growth, improved coloration, and more vigorous polyp expansion. Feeding is considered optional in well-established tanks with stable nutrients, but regular supplemental feeding tends to produce noticeably better results.

goodWith: other peaceful LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: other LPS with potent sweeper tentacles placed in contact
any coral or anemone that makes direct contact

summary: Blastomussa is a genus of LPS corals with large, fleshy polyps generally considered beginner-to-intermediate in care. They tend to prefer low to moderate indirect light and gentle flow, and are generally regarded as one of the more forgiving LPS choices for reef aquariums.
careNotes:
Note: Blastomussa is a small genus (~6 described species) discussed largely at the genus level in the aquarium trade because all species share near-identical husbandry requirements. The most commonly kept species are Blastomussa wellsi (larger polyps, 3–13 cm diameter) and Blastomussa merleti (smaller polyps, up to ~2.5 cm diameter). The scientificName is set to 'Blastomussa sp.' to reflect this trade convention.

Lighting: Blastomussa tends to prefer low to moderate lighting (generally 50–150 PAR), and has been documented bleaching or retracting when exposed to high-intensity lighting. Newly acquired colonies are generally advised to be placed in the lowest, most shaded area of the tank for the first 2–3 weeks, then gradually relocated to their permanent position. Sudden jumps in light intensity should be avoided.

Flow: Low to gently indirect flow is strongly advised. Direct powerhead flow has been known to cause prolonged polyp retraction and, in extended cases, tissue damage. Placing the colony behind live rock or in a naturally sheltered area of the tank allows food delivery while protecting the coral from turbulence.

Aggression: Blastomussa is generally considered peaceful. Their sweeper tentacles are notably weak relative to other LPS corals — multiple sources describe them as having the stinging power of a 'wet noodle.' However, to be safe, leaving at least 5 cm of clear space around colonies is generally advised, as direct contact with any coral or anemone can cause damage to Blastomussa.

Feeding: Although photosynthesis via zooxanthellae provides most of their nutrition, target feeding with mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or specialized coral foods once or twice per week has been widely reported to result in faster growth (estimated 1–4 new polyps per month under optimal conditions), better coloration, and larger polyp size. Zooplankton-sized foods are preferred.

Water chemistry: Blastomussa tends to be more forgiving of minor water parameter fluctuations than SPS corals, but stable alkalinity, calcium, and magnesium are still generally recommended. Supplementing strontium and trace elements is sometimes mentioned but evidence of measurable benefit in well-maintained reef tanks is limited.

Taxonomy note: Blastomussa was historically placed in family Mussidae; current classification per the World Register of Marine Species places it in family Plerogyridae (Order Scleractinia). Some aquarium hobby sources still reference Mussidae.

Fragging: Blastomussa is straightforward to frag in captivity. Because individual polyps sit atop separate corallite skeletons, clean cuts between corallites rarely damage neighboring polyps. Frags heal and attach quickly.

Common color morphs include red, green, and multi-colored (rainbow or tie-dye) variants — some highly pigmented morphs command significant price premiums.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting between individual corallites) is the primary propagation method in captivity and is widely regarded as easy to moderate in difficulty. Individual polyps have their own separate skeleton, making clean cuts straightforward. Sexual reproduction occurs in the wild via broadcast spawning, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – LPS Hard Corals
primary.notes: LiveAquaria collection page confirmed Blastomussa is carried as an LPS coral; individual species product pages were not accessible via direct URL. Husbandry parameters sourced primarily from MetaReef and cross-verified against four additional secondary sources.

additional.name: MetaReef – Blasto Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided: temperature 75–80°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.024–1.026, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, calcium 420–440 ppm, magnesium 1260–1350 ppm, PAR 75–150, placement lower third, flow low-to-moderate, care level beginner-to-intermediate, feeding 1–2×/week. Cross-checked against all other sources.

additional.name: AquariumSource – Blastomussa Coral
additional.notes: Cross-verification source. Gave temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C) — lower range than other sources (averaged in); salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1350 ppm, PAR 75–125, care easy-to-moderate, minimum tank 30 gal (114 L). Minor disagreement on temperature lower bound (22 vs. 23°C) resolved by averaging across all sources.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Blastomussa Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verification source. Gave temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C) — upper range is notably higher than other sources; alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, PAR 75–125 (target 100), care level moderate. Temperature upper bound of 29°C is an outlier; averaged against other sources to reach 27°C max.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – Blastomussa Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-verification source. Gave temperature 76–81°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.2–8.4, alkalinity 8–11 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1350 ppm, minimum tank 30 gal (114 L), placement sandbed/lower rockwork, flow low, care easy-moderate. Confirmed native range: Fiji, Great Barrier Reef, Solomon Islands, Tonga, Indonesia.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Blastomussa (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirmed: Family Plerogyridae (current classification), Order Scleractinia, Genus Blastomussa (Wells, 1968), ~6 recognized species. Not used for husbandry parameters.
